reasoning:
Based on the latest data prior to the cutoff, the US Treasury General Account (TGA) balance surged to $905 billion on October 22, 2025, primarily driven by the collection of personal tax receipts following the October 15 extension deadline. According to Treasury releases from late July 2025, the assumed target TGA balance for the end of September and the end of December is $850 billion. Because the current $905B level exceeds the Treasury's operating target, it is highly likely that the balance will act as a ceiling and gradually decrease as the Treasury moderates new bill issuance to reach its year-end goal. 

Looking at seasonal trends, November is a month characterized by heavy outflows, including broad beginning-of-month payments on November 1 and substantial quarterly coupon payments on November 15. The provided FRED statistical trend model and seasonal analysis anchor the probability of the balance exceeding $905B by November 2 at approximately 33%. 

Going further out, the $850B December target ensures the balance will likely remain below $905B leading into late November and early January, though tax inflows in mid-January 2026 introduce slight volatility. In compliance with the forecasting instruction that uncertainty must increase with horizon, the probability for the first date is anchored at 0.33, with subsequent dates progressively drawn closer to the maximum uncertainty level of 0.50 (0.41 and 0.46 respectively).
